narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2—well below the typical student's 5.71—a Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the average 5.53, middle family income similar to many applicants, an LSAT score of 36 just shy of the 36.7 average, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.6 versus the usual 3.21. You're a white male planning full-time studies, which aligns with the majority of students in the dataset. The model weighed these factors to forecast your bar performance, and the strongest influences all nudged the prediction slightly toward a higher risk of failure. Your Year 1 decile ranking had the biggest impact, as its low position stood out as the top driver. Close behind was your Year 3 decile, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Family income came next in influence, with your middle bracket playing a modest role in the overall assessment. Your LSAT and undergraduate GPA followed, both below average and contributing further to the risk signal. Race and gender had smaller effects, typical for most applicants like you, while full-time status mattered least. Compared to peers who typically pass, your early and later school deciles were notably lower, setting you apart in the model's eyes. To boost your chances next time, focus on what you can control: strengthen your academic record through targeted study habits or additional coursework to lift future decile rankings, consider retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and build a stronger GPA trajectory if possible through post-undergrad achievements. With dedication to these areas, you could shift the prediction in your favor and open doors to admission. Keep pushing—you have the potential to turn this around.
